title: Creating a Believable Persona
description: Step-by-step guide to crafting a backstory that withstands scrutiny.

slides.title: The Persona Blueprint 📝
slides.content:
# Building a Human From Scratch

**Step 1: Define the Role.**
- What job title will you claim? (e.g., 'Senior IT Project Manager')
- Why does this role give you access to the target?

**Step 2: Fill the Timeline.**
- **Birth Year:** Make it plausible for the role (e.g., 1985 for a Senior Manager).
- **Education:** Degree from a large state university (hard to verify specific graduates).
- **Work History:** 2-3 previous jobs. At least one **defunct company** or **massive corporation**.
- **Current Role:** Details of your fake position.

**Step 3: Add Human Details.**
- **Hobbies:** Generic but relatable (e.g., 'Hiking', 'Photography', 'Cooking').
- **Location:** Lives in a nearby city, commutes.
- **Family:** Single, or married with young kids (great excuse for ending calls early).

**Step 4: Document Everything.**
- Create the **Pretext Bible** (a master document or encrypted note).

quizData.question: Why is it advisable to include a defunct company in your fake work history?

options.text: It makes the story more interesting
It prevents verification of employment dates

quizData.explanation: Correct. A company that no longer exists has no HR department to confirm you worked there. It's an unverifiable—and therefore safe—detail.

slides.title: Choosing a Name and Identity 🆔
slides.content:
# The Alias Selection Process

**Name Criteria:**
1.  **Common, not memorable:** 'John Smith' is too obvious. 'David Chen' or 'Sarah Miller' is common enough to be plausible but not a cliché.
2.  **Ethnically Neutral (usually):** Avoid names that trigger unconscious bias or invite questions about 'where you're really from'.
3.  **Matches the Persona:** A 55-year-old CFO shouldn't be named 'Jaxon'.

**Generating a Plausible Alias:**
- Use a **random name generator** that pulls from census data.
- Google the name to ensure it's not associated with a **famous person** or **criminal**.
- Check if the **domain name** is available (e.g., `davidchen-consulting.com`).

**Photo Selection:**
- **DO NOT** use a real person's photo (identity theft).
- Use **AI-generated faces** (e.g., ThisPersonDoesNotExist.com) or **stock photos** (with appropriate licensing).
- The photo should look like a **corporate headshot**—neutral background, professional attire, approachable smile.

slides.title: Example Persona Dossier 📂
slides.content: A complete persona entry in your Pretext Bible:
slides.codeSnippet:
NAME: David Chen
DOB: 03/12/1988 (Age 36)
EDUCATION: B.S. Information Systems, University of Texas at Austin (2010)
WORK HISTORY:
- 2018-Present: Senior IT Project Manager, Apex Solutions (FAKE - our shell company)
- 2015-2018: IT Project Coordinator, Circuit City (DEFUNCT - unverifiable)
- 2010-2015: Junior Sysadmin, Dell Technologies (LARGE CORP - plausible, hard to verify specific employee)
LOCATION: Austin, TX (lives in Round Rock, commutes)
HOBBIES: Cycling, BBQ, Fantasy Football
FAMILY: Married, 2 kids (ages 4 and 6) - good excuse for interruptions
VEHICLE: Silver 2019 Honda Accord (generic, forgettable)
FAVORITE SPORTS TEAM: Texas Longhorns

quizData.question: True or False: It's best to use a real person's LinkedIn photo for your fake persona to make it more believable.
quizData.explanation: False. This is identity theft and illegal. Use AI-generated faces or properly licensed stock photos.

slides.title: The Pretext Bible 📖
slides.content:
# Your Single Source of Truth

**Definition:** A secure, encrypted document containing **every detail** of your persona.

**Contents:**
- Full persona dossier (name, DOB, work history, etc.).
- **Canned answers** to common questions:
  - *"Where did you go to school?"* -> "UT Austin. Hook 'em!"
  - *"What do you do for fun?"* -> "Mostly chase my kids around. You got any?"
  - *"What's your extension?"* -> "It's 4552, but I'm rarely at my desk. Cell is better."
- **Technical details** about your fake company (founded date, number of employees, main product).
- **Relationship Map:** If you have multiple fake personas interacting (e.g., 'David Chen' and 'Sarah Miller' work together), document how they know each other.

**Accessibility:** Keep this document on an **encrypted USB drive** or a secure cloud note (Standard Notes, Cryptee) accessible only during the engagement.

slides.title: The 'Kernel of Truth' Technique 🌱
slides.content:
## Base Your Lies on Reality

The most believable lies contain a **kernel of truth**.

**Example:**
- **Lie:** You're a project manager at a fake company.
- **Truth:** You actually **did** work as a project manager 5 years ago (or you've studied PM methodologies).
- **Result:** When the target asks, *"What methodology do you use? Agile or Waterfall?"* you can answer **authentically** and **confidently**.

**Application:**
- If your persona is a 'network engineer,' spend a week learning basic networking terminology (subnets, VLANs, BGP). You don't need to be a CCIE, just enough to **sound** like one in casual conversation.
- **Jargon is your friend.** Drop a few acronyms correctly, and people assume you're an expert.

slides.title: Avoiding the 'Too Perfect' Trap 🕳️
slides.content:
## Real People Have Flaws

A persona that is **too polished** is suspicious.

**Add Human Imperfections:**
- **Bad Memory:** *"Wait, what was the question again? Sorry, long day."*
- **Minor Inconsistencies:** *"I think I started there in 2015... or was it 2016? One of those."* (But have the **correct** date in your Bible).
- **Self-Deprecation:** *"I'm terrible with names. You'll have to remind me next time we talk."*

**Why This Works:**
- Real people forget details. Perfect recall is a **red flag**.
- Self-deprecation makes you **likable** and **non-threatening**.
- It provides **cover** if you slip up. *"Oh, did I say 2015? My bad, it was 2016."*
